attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about small, dark red spots that have appeared on his trunk and thighs over the past few years. They are non-blanching and not pruiritic. He reports episodes of burning pain in his hands and feet and notices he sweats less than usual. His father died at age 45 from a stroke, and his brother has chronic kidney disease - both was due to the same genetic disease. He takes no regular medications, denies any alcohol intake, and has no other significant medical history.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Multiple small, non-blanching, dark red papules are noted on his trunk, thighs, umbilical region and in his mouth. His heart sounds are normal and there is no peripheral oedema.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most characteristic clinical feature of this genetic disease?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry disease, angiokeratoma corporis diffusum or Anderson-Fabry disease). This is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficient activity of alpha-galactosidase A. It affects multiple organ systems due to accumulation of glycosphingolipids and typically presents in childhood or adolescence. Untreated disease can lead to renal failure, strokes, arrythmia or cardiac failure as occurred in his father and brother.

Angiokeratomas are the characteristic cutaneous manifestation of Fabry disease. They appear as small, dark red to purple, non-blanching papules that typically develop in a bathing trunk distribution. These lesions result from progressive glycosphingolipid deposition in the walls of superficial blood vessels.

Hyperhidrosis (excessive sweating) is not a feature of Fabry disease. In fact, patients typically present with hypohidrosis (reduced sweating) due to damage to sweat glands. Patients may have chronic neuropathic pain as well.

Erythema nodosum presents as tender, erythematous nodules typically on the shins. It is often associated with infections, medications, or inflammatory conditions but is not a feature of Fabry disease.

Pyoderma gangrenosum is a neutrophilic dermatosis characterised by painful, progressive ulceration. It is often associated with inflammatory bowel disease and other systemic conditions, but not with Fabry disease.

Developmental delay is not typically associated with Fabry disease. While the condition can affect multiple organ systems, cognitive development is usually normal.

Other important features of Fabry disease include gastrointestinal symptoms, left ventricular hypertrophy, and stroke. Early recognition is crucial as enzyme replacement therapy can slow disease progression.
learningPoints: Angiokeratomas are a characteristic cutaneous manifestation of Fabry disease, appearing as small dark red spots.
Fabry disease presents with multiple system involvement including neuropathic pain, gastrointestinal symptoms, and renal dysfunction.
references: Jin Q, Oakley A, Mitchell G, McGivern M. Fabry Disease. DermNet NZ. 2019. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/fabry-disease

Kok K, Zwiers KC, Boot RG, Overkleeft HS, Aerts JMFG, Artola M. Fabry Disease: Molecular Basis, Pathophysiology, Diagnostics and Potential Therapeutic Directions. Biomolecules. 2021; 11(2):271. Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2218-273X/11/2/271
Besekar SM, Jogdand SD, Naqvi WM. Fabry Disease and Its Management: A Literature Analysis. Cureus. 2023. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10154914/
